title: R Coding Reference
text: A living reference. Things go here when I learn them and want to find them fast later. Not exhaustive, opinionated about what’s useful for biostat / HEOR work.

title: R Coding Reference
text:
The non-negotiables

R is case-sensitive. Always. Filter ≠ filter. df ≠ DF.
Assignment uses <- by convention, though = works in most places. Inside function calls, = means “named argument,” not “assign.”
== is equality, = is assignment-in-arguments. Mixing them is a classic SAS-programmer bug.
Vectors are 1-indexed, not 0. x[1] is the first element.
NA is contagious. Most arithmetic with NA returns NA. Use na.rm = TRUE in summary functions, or sum(is.na(x)) to count missings.

title: R Coding Reference
text:
Pipes

|> is the base R pipe (R 4.1+). Prefer it for new code.
%>% is the magrittr pipe. Almost identical. Some edge cases differ — %>% supports . placeholder, |> supports _ placeholder (with named args only) starting R 4.2.

title: R Coding Reference
section: Functions vs. macros (the SAS programmer’s mental shift)
text:
Functions vs. macros (the SAS programmer’s mental shift)

Functions in R are first-class objects. You can pass them as arguments, store them in lists, return them from other functions. SAS macros are text substitution; R functions are real values.
Default arguments in functions: f <- function(x, n = 10) .... The n = 10 is the default if not supplied.
The ... (dots) argument captures additional arguments to pass to inner functions. Used heavily in tidyverse and for plotting.

title: R Coding Reference
text:
Common gotchas

Function name conflicts. Loading dplyr masks stats::filter() and stats::lag(). Loading MASS masks dplyr::select(). When in doubt, prefix: dplyr::filter(). The conflicted package makes this explicit and is worth using.
data.frame vs tibble vs data.table. All three are tabular. Tibbles print nicer and don’t do silent type coercion. data.table is fast but a different syntax. For tidyverse work, tibbles. For huge data, data.table or arrow.
Factors. read.csv used to make strings into factors silently. Modern R (4.0+) doesn’t. readr::read_csv and data.table::fread never did. Factors are still useful — just don’t get them by accident.
sapply is a foot-gun. It tries to simplify the output and can return a vector, matrix, or list depending on inputs. Use vapply (type-safe) or purrr::map_* (explicit return type) instead.

title: R Coding Reference
text:
Useful idioms

# Read everything as character to avoid type surprises, then convert deliberately
readr::read_csv("file.csv", col_types = readr::cols(.default = "c"))

# "Fill down" missing values (very common in pivoted data)
tidyr::fill(df, col_name, .direction = "down")

# Per-group operation without group_by/ungroup ceremony (dplyr 1.1+)
df |> mutate(rank = row_number(), .by = subject_id)

# Anti-join: rows in x not in y by key
dplyr::anti_join(x, y, by = "id")

# String interpolation
glue::glue("Subject {usubjid} had Cmax = {round(cmax, 2)}")

title: R Coding Reference
text:
Project setup

Always use an RStudio Project (.Rproj file). Sets the working directory automatically — no more setwd().
here::here() for paths. Resolves from the project root, not from wherever the script happens to be running.
renv for project-level package management. Like a requirements.txt for R. Essential for reproducible analyses.
targets for analysis pipelines. The make-equivalent for R; reruns only what changed. Worth learning for any multi-step project.

title: R Coding Reference
text:
Table 1 / regulatory output

gtsummary::tbl_summary() — the fastest path to a publication-ready Table 1.
gt — the underlying table package; full control.
tfrmt (pharmaverse) — for regulatory-compliant tables matching SAS-style specs.

title: R Coding Reference
text:
Modeling

broom::tidy() turns any model object into a tidy data frame of coefficients. broom::glance() for model-level stats. broom::augment() for fitted/residual values.
marginaleffects is the right way to get adjusted predictions, marginal effects, and contrasts from any model. Replaces emmeans for most use cases I care about.
Always check confint() — base R confidence intervals on coefficients are profile likelihood by default for GLMs, Wald for lm. Specify if you care.

title: R Coding Reference
text:
Things I keep relearning
I’ll add to this list as I keep tripping on things.

The default contrasts for unordered factors are treatment contrasts (dummy coding). For ordered factors, polynomial. Set options(contrasts = c("contr.treatment", "contr.treatment")) if you want consistent behavior.
seq(1, 10, by = 2) not seq(1, 10, 2) — positional args work but named is clearer.
paste() defaults to space separator; paste0() defaults to no separator. Use paste0 for filenames and identifiers.

title: From SAS to R, and into HEOR/RWE
text:
Note



This is a living learning journal, not a textbook. Entries reflect what I’m working through right now — concepts I’m wrestling with, code I’m writing, resources I’m reading, and the takeaways I want my future self to remember. Some entries are polished, some are rough. That’s the point.

title: From SAS to R, and into HEOR/RWE
text:
Why this exists
I’m a clinical biostatistician (PharmD, MPH) currently programming primarily in SAS, transitioning toward R fluency and HEOR/RWE methodology. This site is where I document that transition — partly as a personal reference, partly so anyone walking a similar path has something to follow along with.
The format is applied and project-based. Every entry tries to answer: what is this concept, why does it matter, what does the code look like, what resources helped me, and what do I want to remember next time?

title: From SAS to R, and into HEOR/RWE
text:
The two tracks


SAS → R
Becoming proficient in R as a working biostatistician. R fundamentals, the tidyverse, pharmaverse (admiral, xportr, tfrmt, metacore), and side-by-side translations of SAS patterns I use every day.


HEOR & RWE
Building expertise in health economics and outcomes research and real-world evidence. Statistical models (ZINB, GLMs, survival), causal inference, study design, and the tools and data sources used in the field.

title: From SAS to R, and into HEOR/RWE
text:
Quick links

R coding reference — the running cheat sheet I wish I’d had on day one
Resources reviewed — every book, course, and paper, with my one-line take
About

title: From SAS to R, and into HEOR/RWE
text:
How to use this site
If you’re following along: pick a track and start at its index page. Entries are dated and roughly chronological within each section. If you spot something wrong, the Edit this page link in the right sidebar takes you straight to the source on GitHub.

title: dplyr & tidyverse
text: The grammar of data manipulation in R. Coming from SAS, this is the section that does the most work — proc sql and data steps map onto dplyr in mostly clean ways, but the mental model is different enough to be worth writing down.

title: dplyr & tidyverse
text:
Entries

Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs

More entries as I progress through the 26-week plan.

title: SAS vs R Patterns
text:
Side-by-side translations of patterns I use every day in SAS, written in R. The point isn’t to argue one is better — it’s to make the mental jump faster when I’m in R and reaching for a SAS habit.
Planned entries: proc sort + first./last. → dplyr::group_by + slice, proc transpose → pivot_wider/pivot_longer, retain statements → cumsum/accumulate, merge logic, macro variables → functions and glue.



 Back to top

title: R Fundamentals
text:
The conceptual groundwork — the things that aren’t really about doing analysis but about understanding why R behaves the way it does. Entries here will cover data types, vectors vs. lists, factors, environments, NSE, and the SAS-programmer’s “wait, what?” moments.
Entries will be added here as I work through them. Check back, or follow the repo.



 Back to top

title: What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect
text:
Note



Source: Hernán MA, Robins JM. Causal Inference: What If. Boca Raton: Chapman & Hall/CRC, 2020. Free PDF.
The summary below is in my own words — what I took away, why it matters to me as a biostatistician moving toward HEOR/RWE. For the actual material, read the book.
crumbs: *What If*, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

title: What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect
text:
Concept
A causal effect, defined cleanly, is a contrast between two potential outcomes for the same unit under different treatments. For an individual, the causal effect of treatment \(A\) on outcome \(Y\) is \(Y^{a=1} - Y^{a=0}\) — the difference between what would happen if treated and what would happen if untreated. The fundamental problem of causal inference is that we only ever observe one of these two potential outcomes for each individual. The other is counterfactual.
Because individual causal effects are unidentifiable, causal inference operates at the average level: \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\). We swap the impossible (knowing what would have happened to person \(i\)) for the merely difficult (estimating averages over groups under untestable assumptions).
crumbs: *What If*, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

title: What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect
text:
Why it matters
This chapter is the conceptual fork in the road. Once you accept that:

Causation is defined in terms of counterfactuals, not just associations,
We can never observe both potential outcomes for the same person,
So everything we do in causal inference is built on assumptions that license us to use observed data to estimate averages of unobserved counterfactuals —

…then everything else in the book (and in HEOR/RWE practice) makes sense as machinery for making that swap defensible. Propensity scores, IPTW, target trial emulation, IV — they’re all answers to the question: given that I can’t observe counterfactuals, under what assumptions can I still get \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\) from data I do have?
For my work specifically: this is the lens I need to bring to RWE. A regression with covariates is not a causal analysis just because it controls for things. It becomes one when I can write down the counterfactual contrast it estimates and the assumptions under which my estimator equals that contrast.
crumbs: *What If*, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

title: What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect
section: My summary — key concepts that helped me understand
text:
My summary — key concepts that helped me understand

Potential outcomes notation \(Y^a\). \(Y^{a=1}\) is the outcome a person would have under treatment; \(Y^{a=0}\) is the outcome the same person would have under no treatment. These are properties of the person, not of the data. Most of them are unobserved by definition.
Individual causal effect = \(Y^{a=1} - Y^{a=0}\). Defined for a person. Almost never identifiable. The point of writing it down isn’t to estimate it — it’s to be precise about what you’d want to estimate in a perfect world.
Average causal effect = \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\). This is the population-level quantity we can estimate, given assumptions. It’s not just “the average of individual effects” in any practically useful sense — it’s the contrast of two whole-population means under counterfactual treatment regimes.
Causation ≠ Association. \(E[Y \mid A=1] - E[Y \mid A=0]\) (an associational contrast) is not the same as \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\) (a causal contrast). They’re equal only under conditions like exchangeability, which randomization buys you and observational data does not.
Three measures of effect show up: risk difference, risk ratio, odds ratio. Different scales, different uses. For HEOR work where I’ll be reporting to decision-makers, the risk difference / absolute scale is usually the most communicative; multiplicative measures collapse base-rate information.
Sharp vs. average null hypotheses. “Treatment had no effect on anyone” (sharp null) is much stronger than “average effect is zero.” A non-zero average can hide huge heterogeneity, and a zero average can hide opposite-direction effects in subgroups. This matters for HTE work.
crumbs: *What If*, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

title: What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect
text:
Things to remember

Whenever I hear “the effect of \(A\) on \(Y\),” I should mentally translate to: “the contrast \(E[Y^{a=1}] - E[Y^{a=0}]\), under what assumptions, in what population.” If I can’t fill those blanks in, the claim isn’t well-defined yet.
A causal claim from observational data is a claim about counterfactuals plus a defense of the assumptions that license bridging from observed to counterfactual. The defense is the analysis. The model is just the bookkeeping.
“Confounding” in this framework is precisely the failure of exchangeability: \(Y^a \not\!\perp\!\!\!\perp A\). That’s a much sharper definition than the usual “a third variable that distorts the relationship.”
crumbs: *What If*, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

title: What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect
text:
Code
No code in this chapter — it’s foundational. Code starts showing up around Chapter 11 (IP weighting) and 12 (g-formula). I’ll write up those entries as I get there, with R implementations using survey, WeightIt, and marginaleffects.
crumbs: *What If*, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

title: What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect
text:
Status
Read. Re-read introduction and 1.4 (effect measures). Moving to Chapter 2 (randomized experiments) next.
crumbs: *What If*, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

title: HEOR & RWE
text: The methodological core of HEOR/RWE work — the statistical models, the causal inference frameworks, the data sources, and the software the field actually uses. My anchor project is a ZINB model comparison on MEPS utilization data, targeting ISPOR 2027.

title: HEOR & RWE
text: The methodological core of HEOR/RWE work — the statistical models, the causal inference frameworks, the data sources, and the software the field actually uses. My anchor project is a ZINB model comparison on MEPS utilization data, targeting ISPOR 2027.

title: HEOR & RWE
text:
Sections

Statistical Models
The model families that show up constantly in HEOR/RWE: count models (Poisson, NB, ZIP, ZINB, hurdle), GLMs for cost data, survival, mixed models. Each entry walks through the model, when to use it, the R implementation, and the diagnostics.


Methods & Concepts
Causal inference, target trial emulation, propensity scores, instrumental variables, study design. The “what’s the right question” layer that sits above any specific model.


Tools & Software
The R ecosystem for HEOR/RWE: pscl, survey, countreg, marginaleffects, MatchIt, WeightIt, survival, tidymodels. Setup notes, gotchas, and when to reach for which.


Resources Reviewed
Every book, course, paper, and webinar I’ve worked through, with my one-line take and a link to a deeper summary if I wrote one.

title: HEOR & RWE
text:
Current focus
Working through Hernán & Robins, Causal Inference: What If, Part I. First detailed entry: Chapter 1 — A definition of causal effect.

title: Tools & Software
text:
The R ecosystem for HEOR/RWE work. Setup notes, what each package is for, and the gotchas I hit.
Planned coverage: pscl (zero-inflated/hurdle models), countreg (count model diagnostics), survey (complex survey designs — essential for MEPS), marginaleffects (post-estimation, the way), MatchIt and WeightIt (PS methods), survival and survminer, cmprsk (competing risks), data sources (MEPS, CPRD, SEER-Medicare).



 Back to top

title: Setup guide — get this site live in 30 minutes
text:
Step-by-step from “files on disk” to “public site at github.io.” Assumes you have a GitHub account and a working terminal.


Download from https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/ for your OS. Verify:
quarto --version
You don’t need R installed for the site to render as it’s currently set up — all R chunks have eval: false. You only need R if you want code chunks to actually run during render. (For now, leaving them un-evaluated is fine and faster.)




Go to https://github.com/new.
Name it learning-journey (or whatever you want — just be consistent).
Public. Don’t initialize with README, license, or .gitignore — we already have those.
Create repo. Copy the SSH or HTTPS URL.




From inside the learning-journey/ folder I generated:
git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial scaffold"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin git@github.com:YOURUSERNAME/learning-journey.git
git push -u origin main



Find-and-replace YOURUSERNAME with your actual GitHub username in:

_quarto.yml (site-url, repo-url, footer)
index.qmd (no change needed, but check)
about.qmd
README.md

Same for YOURPROFILE in the LinkedIn footer link.
Commit and push these.




On GitHub, go to your repo → Settings → Pages.
Under Build and deployment, set Source to GitHub Actions.
That’s it — the workflow in .github/workflows/publish.yml will fire on your next push.




Either push a small change, or go to Actions tab → Publish Quarto site → Run workflow.
After 1–2 minutes, your site will be live at:
https://YOURUSERNAME.github.io/learning-journey/



Edit .qmd files in your editor. Preview locally:
quarto preview
This opens a live-reloading browser tab. Edit, save, see changes immediately. When you’re happy:
git add .
git commit -m "Add entry: propensity score matching"
git push
GitHub Actions does the rest. Site updates in ~2 minutes.



cp _entry-template.qmd heor-rwe/methods-concepts/propensity-score-matching.qmd
# edit it
# add it to heor-rwe/methods-concepts/index.qmd's listing
# (optionally) add it to _quarto.yml sidebar
git add . && git commit -m "Add: PS matching entry" && git push




Custom domain later — once you have ~20 entries, point a domain like priya-stats.com at the GitHub Pages site. Settings → Pages → Custom domain.
Analytics — add a Plausible or umami snippet to _quarto.yml under format.html.include-in-header if you eventually want to see traffic.
RSS feed — Quarto can generate one for any listing page. Useful when you start sharing.
Search is already enabled in the navbar config — it indexes everything automatically.




For the first 4–6 weeks:

Site is public and indexable. ✅
Don’t link it from LinkedIn yet. ✅
Aim for one new entry per week, plus updates to the R cheatsheet whenever you learn something.
Build a small backlog (8–10 entries) before any active promotion.
First share moment: after R/Pharma submission, or when you have a Resources Reviewed page that’s actually got reviews on it.

By the time you announce, there’s substance. Until then, it’s quietly accumulating credibility.

My rating scale

5 — Changed how I think about a topic. Will re-read.
4 — Strong, recommended; I learned a lot.
3 — Useful for what it is; reference-grade.
2 — Mixed; some good parts.
1 — Skip.

title: Methods & Concepts
text: The conceptual layer above the models — how to ask the right causal question, how to design a study, how to think about confounding and identification before reaching for an estimator.

title: Methods & Concepts
text:
Entries

What If, Ch. 1 — A definition of causal effect

Planned: target trial emulation, propensity scores (matching vs. weighting vs. stratification), IV methods, regression discontinuity, sensitivity analysis (E-values), study designs in RWE.

title: Statistical Models
text:
Model families I’m building working knowledge of for HEOR/RWE. Each entry follows the same structure: the model, the assumptions, when to use it, R code, diagnostics, and what tripped me up.
Planned: Poisson regression, negative binomial, zero-inflated Poisson, ZINB (the anchor project model), hurdle models, GLMs for cost (gamma, log-link), Cox PH refresher in R, mixed models with lme4.



 Back to top

title: pharmaverse
text:
The R packages built specifically for clinical trial work: admiral (ADaM datasets), xportr (XPT v5 transport files), metacore/metatools (specs and metadata), tfrmt (tables), pharmaversesdtm (test data). This is the stack that makes R viable for regulated submissions.
Entries to come as I incorporate these into real ADaM work.



 Back to top

title: SAS → R
text: I program in SAS daily. R is where the field is moving — pharmaverse, tidyverse, regulatory submissions, and HEOR work all increasingly happen in R. This track is the applied path from “I can read R” to “I’d reach for R first.”

title: SAS → R
text: I program in SAS daily. R is where the field is moving — pharmaverse, tidyverse, regulatory submissions, and HEOR work all increasingly happen in R. This track is the applied path from “I can read R” to “I’d reach for R first.”

title: SAS → R
text:
Sections

R Fundamentals
Data types, environments, scoping, the things that trip up SAS programmers when they first hit R. The “why does this work this way” entries.


dplyr & tidyverse
The core data manipulation grammar. Most of my Week 2–8 R plan lives here — verbs, joins, pivots, list-columns.


pharmaverse
The clinical R stack: admiral for ADaM, xportr for transport files, metacore for specs, tfrmt for tables. This is where R earns its place against SAS in regulated work.


SAS vs R patterns
Side-by-side translations of patterns I use constantly: proc sort + first. / last., proc transpose, retain, merge logic, macro variables. Aimed at SAS programmers — including future me.

title: SAS → R
text:
Current focus
Week 2 of my 26-week plan: dplyr core verbs (select, filter, mutate, arrange, summarize, group_by).

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
text:
dplyr gives you a small set of “verbs” that each do one thing to a data frame and return a data frame. The point is that any data manipulation, no matter how complicated, decomposes into a chain of these verbs piped together with |> (or %>%). That’s the whole grammar.
The six core verbs:








Verb
What it does
SAS analog




select()
Pick columns
keep / drop in a data step; select in proc sql


filter()
Pick rows by condition
where clause; if statement


arrange()
Sort rows
proc sort


mutate()
Add or change columns
assignment in a data step


summarize()
Collapse rows to summary stats
proc means / proc summary


group_by()
Set the grouping for later verbs
by statement

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
text:
dplyr gives you a small set of “verbs” that each do one thing to a data frame and return a data frame. The point is that any data manipulation, no matter how complicated, decomposes into a chain of these verbs piped together with |> (or %>%). That’s the whole grammar.
The six core verbs:








Verb
What it does
SAS analog




select()
Pick columns
keep / drop in a data step; select in proc sql


filter()
Pick rows by condition
where clause; if statement


arrange()
Sort rows
proc sort


mutate()
Add or change columns
assignment in a data step


summarize()
Collapse rows to summary stats
proc means / proc summary


group_by()
Set the grouping for later verbs
by statement

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
text:
Why it matters
In SAS, “manipulating data” usually means writing a data step or a proc sql. The mental model is procedural: read each row, do something, output. In dplyr, the model is transformational: take a table, return a table. Once you internalize that, complex pipelines stop feeling like programming and start feeling like writing.
For ADaM work specifically, this matters because most ADaM derivations are exactly this shape — take SDTM, filter, join, derive, summarize. admiral is built on dplyr.

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
text:
Code: a worked example

library(dplyr)

# A tiny PK-shaped dataset
adpc <- tibble::tribble(
  ~USUBJID,    ~AVISIT,    ~ATPT,     ~AVAL,
  "001",       "Cycle 1",  "Pre-dose", 0.0,
  "001",       "Cycle 1",  "1 hr",     12.4,
  "001",       "Cycle 1",  "4 hr",     8.1,
  "002",       "Cycle 1",  "Pre-dose", 0.0,
  "002",       "Cycle 1",  "1 hr",     14.7,
  "002",       "Cycle 1",  "4 hr",     9.3
)

# Cmax per subject per visit
adpc |>
  filter(ATPT != "Pre-dose") |>
  group_by(USUBJID, AVISIT) |>
  summarize(CMAX = max(AVAL), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(USUBJID, AVISIT)

The pipe |> reads left-to-right: take adpc, then drop pre-dose rows, then group by subject and visit, then collapse to the max, then sort. Each step is a verb. The whole pipeline is one expression.

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
text:
Resources used

R for Data Science (2e), Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel, Grolemund — Chapters 3–5 (free online). The canonical introduction.
dplyr cheatsheet from Posit (PDF). Print it. Tape it up.
vignette("dplyr") — the package’s own intro, runs in the console.

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
section: My summary — the things I want to remember
text:
My summary — the things I want to remember

Pipes pass the left side as the first argument. x |> f(y) is f(x, y). Almost all dplyr verbs take the data frame as the first argument because of the pipe.
group_by() is sticky. Once a tibble is grouped, every subsequent verb respects the grouping until you ungroup() or use .groups = "drop" in summarize(). Forgetting this causes hours of confused debugging.
summarize() collapses, mutate() doesn’t. If you want a per-group statistic added back as a column without collapsing, use mutate() after group_by(), not summarize().
select() can rename in place: select(adpc, subject = USUBJID). Faster than calling rename() separately.
filter() drops NA silently when the condition involves an NA-producing comparison. If you want to keep NAs, use filter(is.na(x) | x > 0).
The .by argument in dplyr 1.1+ lets you do per-call grouping without group_by() + ungroup(): summarize(adpc, CMAX = max(AVAL), .by = c(USUBJID, AVISIT)). Cleaner for one-off aggregations.

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
section: Gotchas / things to remember for coding in R
text:
Gotchas / things to remember for coding in R






Warning



R is case-sensitive. Filter is not filter. USUBJID is not usubjid. SAS forgives case in variable names by default — R never does.



dplyr::filter() is masked by stats::filter() (a time-series function loaded by default). If you ever get bizarre errors from filter(), prefix it as dplyr::filter() to be sure.
summarise() and summarize() are both valid — Wickham gave us both spellings. Pick one and stick with it.
n() only works inside dplyr verbs. It’s not a base R function. nrow(x) is the equivalent for the whole tibble.
Strings: dplyr uses bare column names (NSE), but values are quoted strings. filter(adpc, AVISIT == "Cycle 1") — the column is bare, the comparison value is in quotes.
== for equality, not =. = is assignment-in-an-argument and will not error the way you’d hope; it’ll just behave weirdly.
The pipe |> is base R (4.1+); %>% is from magrittr/dplyr. They’re almost identical but not quite. For new code, prefer |>.

title: Week 2 — Core dplyr verbs
text:
Status
In progress. Next: pivots (pivot_longer/pivot_wider) and joins.

title: About
text: I’m a clinical biostatistician with a PharmD and MPH (biostatistics/epidemiology). I work in oncology, dermatology, urology, and other therapeutic areas — primarily in SAS — and I’m transitioning toward HEOR/RWE methodology with R as my main tool.

title: About
text: I’m a clinical biostatistician with a PharmD and MPH (biostatistics/epidemiology). I work in oncology, dermatology, urology, and other therapeutic areas — primarily in SAS — and I’m transitioning toward HEOR/RWE methodology with R as my main tool.

title: About
text:
What I’m working toward

Short term: R proficiency for clinical and HEOR work; an applied portfolio project (ZINB model comparison on MEPS utilization data).
Medium term: A talk at R/Pharma; a submission to ISPOR; transition into an HEOR/RWE-focused role.
Long term: Senior expertise in real-world evidence methodology.

title: About
text:
Why I’m publishing this
Three reasons. First, writing forces clarity — if I can’t explain a concept, I don’t really understand it. Second, future me will forget things, and a searchable reference saves hours. Third, the path from clinical SAS programming into HEOR/RWE isn’t well-documented for people in my position, and I want what I learn to be useful to the next person.

title: About
text:
How to reach me
GitHub issues on the repo are the best place for corrections or suggestions. For longer conversations, LinkedIn.
